data.content:
The startup crash is **not** caused by rerun — those last two `re_sdk`/`re_grpc_client` lines are just a rerun sink trying (and failing) to flush during shutdown. They're noise triggered *because* the app is already exiting.

The real cause is NATS:

```
File ".../novax/novax.py", line 97, in program_store_lifespan
    await self._nova.open()
File ".../nova/core/nova.py", line 64, in open
    await self.nats.connect(...)
nats.errors.NoServersError: nats: no servers available for connection
```

## Why

`Novax.program_store_lifespan` calls `self._nova.open()` on startup ([novax/novax.py](novax/novax.py#L150)), and `Nova.open()` unconditionally connects to NATS ([nova/core/nova.py](nova/core/nova.py#L64)):

```python
await self.nats.connect(**(self._config.nats_client_config or {}))
```

The NATS server URL is auto-derived from `NOVA_API` in [nova/config.py](nova/config.py#L91):

```python
self.nats_client_config["servers"] = f"ws://{parsed_host.hostname}:{port or 80}/api/nats"
```

Since your app runs in-cluster, `NOVA_API` defaults to `http://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080`, so it tries to open a websocket to `ws://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/nats`. That path returns **HTTP 404** — meaning the api-gateway of the NOVA instance you're deployed against does **not expose the `/api/nats` websocket route**.

Common reasons for the 404:
- The NOVA instance is **older** than the SDK and doesn't have the `/api/nats` gateway route yet (SDK/instance version mismatch).
- NATS / the websocket gateway isn't enabled on that instance.

## How to fix

Pick whichever applies:

1. **Point NATS at the real broker** — set the `NATS_BROKER` env var on your app deployment to the actual NATS websocket/host (this bypasses the derived `/api/nats` URL, see [nova/config.py](nova/config.py#L75)).
2. **Upgrade the NOVA instance** so its api-gateway serves `/api/nats`, or enable the NATS gateway on it.
3. If this app genuinely doesn't need NATS, the SDK currently has **no switch** to skip the NATS connect in `Nova.open()` — startup will always fail without a reachable NATS server. That would need a code change.

Do you want me to (a) verify which NATS route your target instance actually exposes, or (b) add an option to make the NATS connection optional/non-fatal in `Nova.open()`?
data.reasoningText:
The real issue is that `Nova.open()` needs a NATS connection, but the derived NATS websocket gateway URL at `ws://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/nats` is returning a 404, meaning that endpoint doesn't exist in the deployed NOVA instance. That's what's causing the app to crash on startup.

The rerun errors are just noise from a shutdown flush — they're not the actual problem. The NOVA instance either doesn't have the `/api/nats` route exposed, which could mean it's an older version or NATS websocket gateway isn't enabled. To fix this, the instance needs to be updated to expose that endpoint, or I'd need to configure a different NATS broker URL.

data.content: I think nats broker is bascially this: NATS_BROKER=ws://<host>/api/nats

Yes, exactly right. The format is `NATS_BROKER=ws://<host>/api/nats` — that's precisely what the SDK auto-derives for an `http` host in [nova/config.py](nova/config.py#L91):

```python
self.nats_client_config["servers"] = f"ws://{parsed_host.hostname}:{port or 80}/api/nats"
```

So `NATS_BROKER` just lets you override that derived URL. Setting it takes precedence over the derivation (see [nova/config.py](nova/config.py#L75)).

Two things worth noting for your case:

1. **The 404 in the cluster** means the *internal* endpoint `api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/nats` doesn't serve the NATS websocket route. Pointing `NATS_BROKER` at the **external ingress host** (like your `ws://172.31.13.176/api/nats`) is the correct workaround — the ingress routes `/api/nats` even though the in-cluster service on port 8080 doesn't.

2. In your current `.env`, `NOVA_API=http://172.31.11.175` but the commented `NATS_BROKER` uses `172.31.13.176` — different IPs. Make sure that's intentional; normally NATS lives behind the same gateway host as `NOVA_API`, so you'd likely want `ws://172.31.11.175/api/nats` (or whichever host actually exposes `/api/nats`).
data.reasoningText:
The user is showing me their `.env` file where `NATS_BROKER` is commented out and `NOVA_API` points to a local IP. I'm realizing the error came from an in-cluster pod, not this local environment, so these configs might not be directly related. They're asking me to confirm that `NATS_BROKER` follows the pattern `ws://<host>/api/nats`, and I'm starting to trace through how the config gets derived for HTTP connections.

The websocket URL format `ws://<host>/api/nats` is correct — that's what gets derived for HTTP schemes. But I'm noticing their example uses a different IP (172.31.13.176) than their NOVA_API (172.31.11.175), which could be intentional or a mistake. The real issue is that even with the right format, the 404 error in the cluster suggests the endpoint either doesn't exist on that instance or the internal path is different when accessed from within the cluster versus through an external ingress.

data.content:
Both "other errors" come from **rerun**, and they trace to one line in your program.

## 1. The rerun errors

Your [start_here.py](examples/your-nova-app/your_nova_app/start_here.py#L39) declares:

```python
viewer=nova.viewers.Rerun(),  # add this line for a 3D visualization
```

`nova.viewers.Rerun()` defaults to `spawn=True` ([nova/viewers/rerun.py](nova/viewers/rerun.py#L60)), so on startup it tries to launch a Rerun viewer process inside the pod. That container is headless, hence:

- `winit EventLoopError: neither WAYLAND_DISPLAY nor WAYLAND_SOCKET nor DISPLAY is set` — no display.
- `Spawned Rerun Viewer did not bind to port 9876 in time` — the viewer never came up.
- The `re_sdk`/`re_grpc_client` flush errors from before — the same viewer failing to connect on shutdown.

Since you don't need rerun in-cluster, remove/disable that viewer. Let me comment it out:

I'm seeing there are two separate issues at play here. The first one involves the Rerun viewer trying to spawn a process in a headless container environment, which fails because there's no display server available—the windowing system can't initialize without WAYLAND_DISPLAY or X11 support.

The second issue is about NATS derivation from NOVA_API. The URL is being derived correctly from the internal gateway service, but the gateway itself doesn't actually expose the NATS websocket endpoint at that path and port. So the derivation logic is working as intended, but the underlying infrastructure doesn't support what's being requested—the user might be expecting the external gateway behavior rather than what the in-cluster service provides. I should focus on the concrete fix: removing the Rerun viewer from the code since it's not needed in-cluster and is causing those errors. I'll make that edit to start_here.py rather than just diagnosing, since that's the clear solution here.

data.content:
## 2. The NATS 404 — it *is* derived from NOVA_API

The derivation is working correctly. Your deployment sets ([k8s/deployment.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/deployment.yaml#L44)):

```yaml
- name: NOVA_API
  value: "http://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080"
```

and the SDK derives NATS straight from it ([nova/config.py](nova/config.py#L91)):

```python
# host = api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local, port = 8080
f"ws://{parsed_host.hostname}:{port or 80}/api/nats"
# -> ws://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/nats
```

So NATS **is** derived from `NOVA_API` exactly as intended. The `404` is not a derivation bug — it means the **internal** api-gateway service on port `8080` does not route `/api/nats`, even though the **external ingress** (`ws://172.31.13.176/api/nats`, port 80) does.

That points to one of:
- The internal api-gateway doesn't proxy the NATS websocket on `:8080` (only the ingress does), or
- In-cluster NATS is a separate service (e.g. its own `nats`/`api-gateway` route on a different port/path).

To confirm what actually serves `/api/nats` inside the cluster, check the gateway/ingress routing. A quick test from inside the pod or cluster:

```bash
kubectl -n wandelbots get svc | grep -i 'gateway\|nats'
kubectl -n cell exec deploy/your-nova-app -- \
  python -c "import urllib.request; print(urllib.request.urlopen('http://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/nats').status)"
```

If the internal gateway genuinely doesn't expose `/api/nats`, set `NATS_BROKER` in the deployment to whatever internal address does (e.g. the ingress host, or the dedicated NATS service), which overrides the derived URL ([nova/config.py](nova/config.py#L75)).

Do you want me to add a `NATS_BROKER` env entry to [k8s/deployment.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/deployment.yaml) once you confirm the correct in-cluster NATS address?
data.reasoningText: NATS is actually derived from NOVA_API, so that connection checks out—the 404 error we're seeing is just an infrastructure routing problem.

data.reasoningText:
I'm noticing the deployment manifest ends around line 52 with just a Service definition, but there's no Ingress resource. That's puzzling because the NOVA home screen typically discovers apps through Ingress annotations or gateway registration. Looking at the code, it seems the app registration to the home screen probably happens during startup through the program store registration mechanism rather than through Ingress discovery.

But here's the real issue: the app is crashing immediately on startup due to a NATS connection error, which means the pod is stuck in a CrashLoop and never reaches a Ready state. Even if there were an Ingress configured, the app wouldn't be routable or visible on the home screen while it's failing to start. Let me check the README to understand how the app is supposed to register with the home screen.

data.content:
Two reasons, and they compound:

**1. Skaffold alone doesn't create the home-screen tile.** Your [skaffold.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/skaffold.yaml) only deploys a `Deployment` + `Service` ([k8s/deployment.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/deployment.yaml), [k8s/service.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/service.yaml)) — there's **no Ingress / app registration**. The home-screen icon (like Signals, Robot Pad, etc.) is created by the NOVA **app-manager**, which only runs when you do `nova app install`. The README says this explicitly:

> Skaffold owns the `Deployment`/`Service` here (it does **not** go through `nova app install`)... if the platform needs its own ingress/route to start the program, run `nova app install` once to establish it, then iterate with `skaffold dev`.

So `skaffold dev` hot-reloads the code, but it never registers the app as a home-screen entry.

**2. The pod is crash-looping anyway.** Even the "visible via startup registration to the store" path can't happen, because startup aborts at the NATS `NoServersError` (`Application startup failed. Exiting.`). A pod that never finishes startup registers nothing.

## Fix order

1. **Get startup to succeed first** — resolve the NATS 404 (set `NATS_BROKER` to the address that actually serves `/api/nats` in-cluster, or fix the gateway route). Until the lifespan completes, nothing registers.
2. **Run `nova app install` once** to create the home-screen tile + ingress/route.
3. Then use `skaffold dev` to iterate with hot reload.

Want me to walk through `nova app install` for this app, or help pin down the correct in-cluster NATS address so startup succeeds?
data.reasoningText: The README clarifies the issue: the home screen tile only appears after the app registers with the store during startup, which fails when NATS isn't available. The docs also note that if the platform needs its own ingress to start, I need to run `nova app install` first to set up the ingress and register the app tile—Skaffold alone only handles the Deployment and Service, not the ingress or registration.

data.content:
Confirmed against the cluster. The correct internal NATS route is the **`nats` service** in the `wandelbots` namespace, not the `api-gateway`:

```
ws://nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080
```

Details:
- The `nats` service exposes port `4222` (native NATS) and `8080` named **`websocket`**.
- A websocket upgrade test returned `101 Switching Protocols` on both `/` and `/api/nats` — NATS ignores the path, so either works.
- Your failing URL was `ws://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/nats` → the **api-gateway** service doesn't proxy `/api/nats` internally (that's an ingress-only route), which is why you got `404`.

## Fix

Set `NATS_BROKER` explicitly in the deployment so it bypasses the (wrong) derived gateway URL. Let me add it to [k8s/deployment.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/deployment.yaml):

3. **Home-screen tile itself** still requires `nova app install` once — Skaffold only deploys the `Deployment`/`Service`, not the app registration/ingress.

That's the key difference — and it's the `App` custom resource, not anything you're missing.

## Why novaflow doesn't need `nova app install`

novaflow's `skaffold dev` deploys via its **Helm chart**, which renders a NOVA app-manager custom resource for each action ([helm/templates/apps/action.yaml](../novaflow/helm/templates/apps/action.yaml)):

```yaml
apiVersion: wandelbots.com/v1alpha1
kind: App
metadata:
  name: action-{{ $name }}
spec:
  appIcon: ../controlplane/static/hiddenapp.png
  image: "...:tag"
  cellName: ...
  port: 8000
  environment:
    - name: NATS_BROKER
      value: "nats://nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:4222"
```

The **app-manager operator** watches these `App` resources and, for each one, creates the Deployment + Service + **ingress/route + the home-screen tile** automatically. That's literally what `nova app install` does under the hood — it registers an `App` resource. novaflow just does the same thing declaratively through Helm, so skaffold alone is enough.

Your `your-nova-app` example instead ships **raw `Deployment` + `Service`** ([k8s/deployment.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/deployment.yaml), [k8s/service.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/service.yaml)). The app-manager never sees an `App` CR, so no tile appears — hence the README's "run `nova app install` once."

(Side note: this also confirms the NATS service — novaflow uses `nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local` too, just the native `nats://...:4222` port since it's not a websocket client. Your Python SDK needs the websocket port `8080`, which is what I set.)

## To make skaffold register the tile (no `nova app install`)

Replace the raw `Deployment`/`Service` in the skaffold manifests with an `App` custom resource, the same way novaflow does. Want me to add a `k8s/app.yaml` with a `wandelbots.com/v1alpha1 App` for `your-nova-app` and point [skaffold.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/skaffold.yaml) at it? I'd base it on the novaflow template (image `your-nova-app` so skaffold substitutes the tag, `port: 3000`, `NATS_BROKER` set to the websocket endpoint).
data.reasoningText:
So novaflow connects to NATS on the native protocol at port 4222 rather than the websocket port—that aligns with my earlier finding about the Python SDK using websocket on 8080. The key difference is that novaflow's Go client uses the native NATS connection. Looking at the main answer now: novaflow doesn't require `nova app install` because its Helm chart, deployed through skaffold, already creates the necessary Wandelbots custom resources.

The app-manager operator then reconciles those resources and automatically generates the deployment, ingress, and home-screen tile. The `nova app install` command just creates that same custom resource declaratively. In contrast, your-nova-app ships raw Kubernetes manifests—Deployment and Service—which the app-manager doesn't recognize, so no tile appears. To fix this with skaffold alone, I'd need to add an `App` custom resource manifest to your-nova-app instead of relying on raw manifests. The CR needs fields like kind, spec.image, cellName, port, appIcon, and environment. Since skaffold handles image substitution across any manifest field that references the artifact image name, using `spec.image: your-nova-app` should work with skaffold's tag replacement. I can create this manifest for you if you'd like.

Looking at the App CRD spec, I notice it has fields like appIcon, cellName, diagnosisPath, environment, and others, but there's no `command`, `args`, or `urlPath` field. That's odd since novaflow's templates reference `urlPath` — either the CRD version in the cluster differs from what the chart expects, or `urlPath` gets handled elsewhere and silently ignored.

Actually, thinking about this more carefully: the CRD in the deployed cluster is what matters for skaffold dev. If `urlPath` isn't there, the app-manager probably derives the URL path automatically from cellName and app name. And without a `command`/`args` field in the App CR, the Deployment uses whatever entrypoint and CMD are baked into the image itself.

This creates a real problem for hot-reload with skaffold — if the Dockerfile's default CMD runs the app normally without `--reload`, then file sync alone won't trigger hot-reloading. I need to check what the current Dockerfile actually specifies as its CMD.

toolRequests.name: read_file
toolRequests.arguments: {"endLine": 120, "filePath": "/Users/cbiering/home/dev/wandelbots/novaflow/skaffold.yaml", "startLine": 1}

data.reasoningText:
The Dockerfile runs uvicorn without reload by default, but the skaffold deployment overrides this with `--reload` for hot reloading. If we switch to an App CR instead, we lose that command override, which means losing the hot reload capability — so there's a real tradeoff between the two approaches.

I'm weighing three options: stick with the App CR alone (gets the tile but no hot reload, requiring rebuilds for each change), run both an App CR and raw Deployment together (creates conflicts with duplicate pods and ingress), or modify the image entrypoint to detect a dev environment variable and enable reload automatically when the App CR's deployment runs. The third option would let us have both the tile and hot reload if we can make the entrypoint smart enough to switch modes.

Option C seems cleanest, but I'm second-guessing whether I'm overcomplicating this. The user's core ask is just getting the action node on the homescreen with skaffold dev—they haven't complained about losing hot reload. That said, the whole existing setup was built around hot reload, so dropping it would be a step backward. I should look at how novaflow actually handles this with their App CRs and skaffold to see if there's a pattern I'm missing.

The question is whether to keep those old YAML files around as reference or clean them up entirely — leaving orphaned files feels messy, but deleting them is permanent.

The app-manager creates an ingress at `/{cellName}/{appName}` based on the App CR's metadata name, so if the app is called `your-nova-app`, the ingress path becomes `/cell/your-nova-app`. The app itself needs to know its root path to serve assets correctly, so BASE_PATH should match that ingress path. {"filePath": "/Users/cbiering/home/dev/wandelbots/wandelbots-nova/examples/your-nova-app/k8s/app.yaml", "content": "# NOVA App custom resource. The in-cluster app-operator reconciles this into a
# Deployment + Service + Ingress AND registers the home-screen tile -- this is the
# same thing `nova app install` creates, so `skaffold dev` alone is enough (no
# separate install step needed).
apiVersion: wandelbots.com/v1alpha1
kind: App
metadata:
  name: your-nova-app
  namespace: cell # <-- change to your cell's namespace
  labels:
    app: your-nova-app
  annotations:
    description: "Your Nova App - serves robot programs"
spec:
  # Icon shown on the NOVA home screen. Served by the app at {basePath}/app_icon.png
  # (see your_nova_app/register_programs.py), so a base-path-relative name works.
  appIcon: app_icon.png
  # Skaffold substitutes the built + pushed image tag here via the resourceSelector
  # entry in skaffold.yaml (default image detection only covers built-in kinds).
  image: your-nova-app
  cellName: cell
  # Port uvicorn listens on (see register_programs.main()).
  port: 3000
  # The operator injects BASE_PATH (=/<cell>/<app>) and creates the route; the app
  # reads it via `root_path=BASE_PATH`, so it must NOT be set here.
  environment:
    - name: CELL_NAME
      value: "cell"
    - name: LOG_LEVEL
      value: "info"
    # NOVA_API is the in-cluster gateway. The SDK derives NATS from it, but the
    # internal gateway does not proxy /api/nats, so NATS_BROKER is set explicitly
    # to the NATS service's websocket port instead.
    - name: NOVA_API
      value: "http://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080"
    - name: NATS_BROKER
      value: "ws://nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080"
    # Run uvicorn with --reload so Skaffold file syncs hot-reload the app in-cluster.
    # Remove (or set to "false") for a production-style deploy.
    - name: DEV_RELOAD
      value: "true"
"}

arguments.content:
# NOVA App custom resource. The in-cluster app-operator reconciles this into a
# Deployment + Service + Ingress AND registers the home-screen tile -- this is the
# same thing `nova app install` creates, so `skaffold dev` alone is enough (no
# separate install step needed).
apiVersion: wandelbots.com/v1alpha1
kind: App
metadata:
  name: your-nova-app
  namespace: cell # <-- change to your cell's namespace
  labels:
    app: your-nova-app
  annotations:
    description: "Your Nova App - serves robot programs"
spec:
  # Icon shown on the NOVA home screen. Served by the app at {basePath}/app_icon.png
  # (see your_nova_app/register_programs.py), so a base-path-relative name works.
  appIcon: app_icon.png
  # Skaffold substitutes the built + pushed image tag here via the resourceSelector
  # entry in skaffold.yaml (default image detection only covers built-in kinds).
  image: your-nova-app
  cellName: cell
  # Port uvicorn listens on (see register_programs.main()).
  port: 3000
  # The operator injects BASE_PATH (=/<cell>/<app>) and creates the route; the app
  # reads it via `root_path=BASE_PATH`, so it must NOT be set here.
  environment:
    - name: CELL_NAME
      value: "cell"
    - name: LOG_LEVEL
      value: "info"
    # NOVA_API is the in-cluster gateway. The SDK derives NATS from it, but the
    # internal gateway does not proxy /api/nats, so NATS_BROKER is set explicitly
    # to the NATS service's websocket port instead.
    - name: NOVA_API
      value: "http://api-gateway.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080"
    - name: NATS_BROKER
      value: "ws://nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080"
    # Run uvicorn with --reload so Skaffold file syncs hot-reload the app in-cluster.
    # Remove (or set to "false") for a production-style deploy.
    - name: DEV_RELOAD
      value: "true"

            port=port,
            reload=True,
            reload_dirs=["/app/your_nova_app"],
            log_level="info",
            proxy_headers=True,
            forwarded_allow_ips="*",
        )

        reload=False,
        log_level="info",

        forwarded_allow_ips="*",
    )

data.content:
Done. Here's what I changed so `skaffold dev` registers the home-screen tile without `nova app install`, matching novaflow's model:

**1. New App custom resource — [k8s/app.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/app.yaml)**
A `wandelbots.com/v1alpha1 App` (same kind novaflow's Helm chart renders). The in-cluster app-operator reconciles it into Deployment + Service + Ingress **and** registers the tile. Includes the required fields (`appIcon`, `cellName`, `image`, `port`) plus env: `NOVA_API`, the correct `NATS_BROKER=ws://nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080`, `CELL_NAME`, `LOG_LEVEL`, and `DEV_RELOAD=true`.

**2. skaffold.yaml** — points `manifests.rawYaml` at `k8s/app.yaml` and adds a `resourceSelector` so Skaffold substitutes the built image tag into the CRD's `.spec.image` (default detection only handles built-in kinds). Verified with `skaffold diagnose`.

**3. register_programs.py** — since the operator owns the Deployment and uses the image's default command (no `--reload` args), `main()` now opts into `uvicorn --reload` via `DEV_RELOAD`, so Skaffold file-sync still hot-reloads.

**4. README** — updated the dev note to reflect the App CR flow.

Two things to be aware of:
- I followed novaflow's proven pattern and left **`BASE_PATH` out** of the App CR — the operator injects `/<cell>/<app>` (that's how `robot-pad` gets `BASE_PATH=/cell/robot-pad`). If the tile 404s, that assumption is the first thing to check.
- The old [k8s/deployment.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/deployment.yaml) / [k8s/service.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/k8s/service.yaml) are now unreferenced (kept as reference). Say the word if you want them deleted.

With `skaffold dev` running, saving these files will redeploy; the app should appear on the home screen once the operator reconciles the `App`.

- The old k8s/deployment.yaml / k8s/service.yaml are now unreferenced (kept as reference). Say the word if you want them deleted.

data.content:
Here's a PR description covering the `fix/nova-app-template` branch:

```markdown
## fix: modernize the nova-app template with in-cluster Skaffold dev + program directory scanning

### Summary
Brings the `your-nova-app` template up to the current SDK (`>=5.7.4,<6`) and makes the
in-cluster dev loop actually work end-to-end: `skaffold dev` now builds, deploys, and
registers the app as a NOVA home-screen tile with hot reload — **no `nova app install`
step required**. Also adds first-class "drop a file in `programs/`" registration to NOVAx.

### Motivation
The old template targeted an outdated SDK and shipped raw `Deployment`/`Service`
manifests. That meant:
- The app never appeared on the home screen (nothing creates the tile/ingress).
- Startup crashed in-cluster: NATS was derived as
  `ws://api-gateway…:8080/api/nats`, which the internal gateway does **not** proxy (404).
- The example program spawned a Rerun viewer that crashed in the headless pod.

### Changes

**NOVAx SDK**
- `Novax(programs="programs")`: scans a directory and imports every `.py` under it
  (recursively) so decorated programs self-register — drop a file in, it's picked up.
  Underscore files (`__init__.py`) are skipped; a missing dir is ignored; `programs=None`
  disables it. Plain imports and `register_module(...)` still work and can be combined.
- New `Novax.scan_programs()`; `serve()` gains `programs` and `static_dir` params.
- `_import_module(..., name=...)` gives scanned files collision-free module names.
- `novax run` CLI: the target arg is now optional — with no target it just scans
  `programs/` and serves.
- Added unit tests for `scan_programs` (recursion, underscore-skipping, registration).

**App template (`examples/your-nova-app`)**
- Replaced raw `Deployment`/`Service` with a `wandelbots.com/v1alpha1` **`App`** custom
  resource (`k8s/app.yaml`). The in-cluster app-operator reconciles it into
  Deployment/Service/Ingress **and** registers the home-screen tile — the same thing
  `nova app install` does — so `skaffold dev` alone is enough.
- Added `resourceSelector` in `skaffold.yaml` so Skaffold substitutes the built image
  tag into the CRD's `.spec.image`.
- Set `NATS_BROKER=ws://nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080` (the NATS service's
  websocket port) instead of the un-proxied gateway path.
- Preserved hot reload under the operator-managed pod: `DEV_RELOAD=true` makes the
  entrypoint run `uvicorn --reload`, and Skaffold file-sync copies changed program files
  into the pod.
- Disabled the Rerun viewer in `start_here.py` (crashes in a headless container).
- Dockerfile: `INSTALL_DEV` build arg pulls in `watchfiles` for the reload loop.
- `pyproject.toml`: bumped SDK, `package = true`, added `app` script and `watchfiles`
  dev dep; added `.env.example`; removed obsolete `deployment.yaml`/`service.yaml`.

**Docs**
- Top-level README: added a "Registering programs" section (scan / import / explicit)
  and refreshed the TOC.
- Template README: documented the `App`-CR + Skaffold dev flow.

### Testing
- `PYTHONPATH=. uv run pytest -rs -v tests/novax/test_auto_register.py`
- `skaffold diagnose` validates the config and `resourceSelector`.
- Verified against a live cell: the NATS websocket endpoint accepts upgrades
  (`101 Switching Protocols`) on `nats.wandelbots.svc.cluster.local:8080`.

### Notes
- `BASE_PATH` is intentionally **not** set in `k8s/app.yaml`; the app-operator injects
  `/<cell>/<app>` (matching how built-in apps like `robot-pad` are wired). If the tile
  404s, that's the first thing to check.
```

data.reasoningText:
I'm noticing there are some test coverage gaps: no CLI test for the optional target parameter in `novax run`, no direct test for handling module name collisions across different directories, and no test for the `serve` function with custom programs and static directories. Given that the user specifically asked me to review whether we should add tests for these branch changes, I should identify which gaps would provide the most value and fill those rather than over-engineering.

The filename collision case is the most important—it's the exact reason the `_import_module` function got the `name` parameter in the first place, and without this fix, files like `programs/a/x.py` and `programs/b/x.py` would collide. Testing that same-named files in different subdirectories both register correctly would validate the fix and is currently untested. The CLI behavior change for optional targets is worth a smaller test too, though it would require mocking `Novax.serve`. The `serve` function itself is harder to test fully since it calls `uvicorn.run` which blocks, so that's lower priority.

I'll focus on adding a collision test to `test_auto_register.py` and a dedicated CLI test file to validate the optional-target change. The template's `register_programs.main()` and the NATS config derivation weren't actually changed in this branch, so those don't need tests.

For the collision test, I need to verify that `scan_programs` generates unique module names by combining the directory structure — `a/x.py` becomes `novax._scanned_programs.a.x` while `b/x.py` becomes `novax._scanned_programs.b.x`, so there's no collision even if both files define programs with the same id. The key is checking how `_import_module` handles the caching by name to ensure the test is valid.

Coverage for `scan_programs` is already solid (8 tests). But two branch behaviors are untested and worth covering:

1. **Filename collisions across subdirectories** — the exact reason `_import_module` gained the `name=` param. Without it, `a/prog.py` and `b/prog.py` share `stem="prog"` and the second hits the module cache, silently dropping its programs. Not currently tested.
2. **`novax run` with no target** (the CLI change making `target` optional).

def test_scan_programs_same_filename_in_different_subdirs(tmp_path):
    # Two files sharing a stem ("prog") must both register: scanning names each
    # module after its location, so the second does not collide with the first
    # in ``sys.modules`` and get silently skipped.
    programs_dir = tmp_path / "programs"
    _write_program(programs_dir / "a", "prog.py", "scan_a_prog")
    _write_program(programs_dir / "b", "prog.py", "scan_b_prog")

    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=str(programs_dir))
    registered = novax.scan_programs()

    assert "scan_a_prog" in registered
    assert "scan_b_prog" in registered

def test_scan_programs_same_filename_in_different_subdirs(tmp_path):

    assert "scan_b_prog" in registered

arguments.content:
import os
import sys

import pytest

import novax.novax as novax_module
from novax.cli import main


@pytest.fixture
def record_serve(monkeypatch):
    """Replace ``Novax.serve`` with a recorder so the CLI never starts uvicorn."""
    calls: dict = {}

    def fake_serve(self, host="0.0.0.0", port=3000, **kwargs):
        calls["host"] = host
        calls["port"] = port

    monkeypatch.setattr(novax_module.Novax, "serve", fake_serve)
    return calls


def test_cli_run_without_target_skips_import(monkeypatch, record_serve):
    imported: list = []
    monkeypatch.setattr(novax_module, "_import_module", lambda *a, **k: imported.append(a))
    monkeypatch.setattr(sys, "argv", ["novax", "run"])

    main()

    # No target -> nothing imported explicitly; serving still happens (scan is in serve()).
    assert imported == []
    assert record_serve["host"] == "0.0.0.0"
    assert record_serve["port"] == 3000


def test_cli_run_with_target_imports_it(monkeypatch, record_serve):
    imported: list = []
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        novax_module, "_import_module", lambda target, *a, **k: imported.append(target)
    )
    monkeypatch.setattr(sys, "argv", ["novax", "run", "pkg/one.py", "--port", "1234"])

    main()

    assert imported == ["pkg/one.py"]
    assert record_serve["port"] == 1234


def test_cli_run_sets_cell_env(monkeypatch, record_serve):
    # Baseline via setenv so monkeypatch restores CELL_NAME after the test.
    monkeypatch.setenv("CELL_NAME", "original")
    monkeypatch.setattr(novax_module, "_import_module", lambda *a, **k: None)
    monkeypatch.setattr(sys, "argv", ["novax", "run", "--cell", "mycell"])

    main()

    assert os.environ["CELL_NAME"] == "mycell"

data.content:
Reviewed test coverage against the branch changes. Here's the assessment:

**Already well covered** (existing `test_auto_register.py`): `scan_programs` — registration, recursion, underscore-skipping, missing dir, `programs=None` disable, directory-arg override, keeping externally-imported programs, and constructor-with-app scanning. Solid.

**Gaps I filled** — two behaviors this branch introduced that had no test:

1. **Filename collisions across subdirectories** — added `test_scan_programs_same_filename_in_different_subdirs` in [tests/novax/test_auto_register.py](tests/novax/test_auto_register.py). This is the specific reason `_import_module` got the `name=` param: without location-based module names, `a/prog.py` and `b/prog.py` share `stem="prog"`, the second hits the `sys.modules` cache, and its programs are silently dropped. The test asserts both register.

2. **`novax run` with optional target** — added [tests/novax/test_cli.py](tests/novax/test_cli.py) (3 tests): no target skips the explicit import (but still serves), a target gets imported, and `--cell` sets `CELL_NAME`. `serve()` is mocked so uvicorn never starts.

**Intentionally not tested** (low value / high effort):
- `serve(static_dir=...)` mounting and `main()`'s `DEV_RELOAD` branch — these just start uvicorn; testing them means mocking the server for little behavioral gain.
- The k8s/skaffold/NATS changes are config, not code (and `nova/config.py` wasn't modified on this branch), so no unit test applies.

All 29 tests pass, formatted and lint-clean.

data.reasoningText: I need to understand why we have both `scan_programs()` and `auto_register()` methods—this seems to relate to the double registration concern that came up in the Copilot review. Let me look at how these are currently implemented in novax.py to give an accurate explanation.

data.content:
They live at **two different layers** of the registration flow:

**`auto_register()` — the sync step (registry → manager).**
When you decorate a function with `@nova.program`, it adds itself to a process-global registry ([get_registered_programs](novax/novax.py#L91)). `auto_register()` just walks that global registry and copies each program into this `Novax` instance's `ProgramManager`. It imports nothing — it only syncs what's already been imported.

**`scan_programs()` — the discovery step (files → registry → manager).**
It `import`s every `.py` under the `programs/` directory so their `@nova.program` decorators actually *run* (that's what puts them in the global registry). It then finishes by calling `auto_register()` to sync them through.

So the relationship is: `scan_programs` is a front-end that *produces* registrations by importing files; `auto_register` is the shared primitive that *consumes* the registry. They exist separately because programs reach the registry through more than one route:

| Route | How it gets into the registry | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Drop a file in `programs/` | `scan_programs()` imports it | `scan_programs` → `auto_register` |
| `import my_pkg.foo` anywhere | import side-effect | `auto_register` |
| `novax.register_module("pkg")` | explicit import | `register_module` → `auto_register` |

`auto_register` is the common final step for all three; `scan_programs` is only needed for the directory-convention route.

---

**On the Copilot review comment:** it's right that in the constructor `scan_programs()` (which already ends in `auto_register()`) followed by a standalone `auto_register()` walks the registry twice. It's harmless (registration is idempotent — re-registering the same id just replaces it), but redundant.

One caveat with its *suggested* fix: `scan_programs()` returns early **without** calling `auto_register()` when `programs=None` (scanning disabled) — see [scan_programs](novax/novax.py#L122). So if you simply drop the trailing `auto_register()`, then `Novax(app, programs=None)` combined with plain imports would no longer register those programs. The safe way to dedupe is to make `scan_programs()` always end in `auto_register()` (even on the `None`/missing-dir path), then remove the extra call.

Want me to apply that de-duplication safely?
data.reasoningText:
I see the distinction now—`auto_register()` syncs programs already in the global registry into the ProgramManager, while `scan_programs()` actually imports Python files from a directory to trigger their decorators and populate that registry first, then calls `auto_register()` to complete the sync. They're operating at different levels: one handles discovery and importing, the other handles the registration handoff.

The Copilot comment makes sense too—calling both `scan_programs()` and then `auto_register()` again in `__init__` is redundant since `scan_programs()` already ends with `auto_register()`, though it's harmless since registering the same program ID just replaces the existing entry. The real answer to why both exist is that `auto_register()` is the primitive needed for other import paths (plain imports, register_module), while `scan_programs()` is the convenience wrapper for the common case of dropping files in a directory. The double auto_register call only matters when scanning is disabled—in that case, scan_programs returns early without calling auto_register, so the standalone call is essential to register plain-imported programs. When scanning is enabled, the trailing auto_register becomes redundant since scan_programs already handles it, but it's harmless to keep for the disabled-scanning scenario. The real issue is that the suggestion would break the case where someone imports programs manually without scanning — they'd expect auto_register to pick them up, but it wouldn't. The cleaner solution is to ensure scan_programs always calls auto_register at the end, regardless of whether scanning actually happened, so it registers everything that's been imported so far. Then the initialization can just call scan_programs once followed by include_programs_router, eliminating the redundant walk without losing any behavior.

However, this changes scan_programs' documented contract — it's supposed to be a no-op returning an empty list when programs=None, but now it would return whatever auto_register finds. The existing test expects scan_programs() with programs disabled to return [], and it would still pass in that specific test since the only program is in an ignored directory that never gets imported, leaving the registry empty.

The real question here is about the design: why have both scan_programs and auto_register at all? I should focus on explaining that two-layer approach clearly, then briefly note the review comment's concern and why the naive fix has this subtle semantic issue.

replacements.oldString:
def scan_programs(self, directory: str | Path | None = None) -> list[str]:
        """Import every program module under a directory so their ``@nova.program``
        functions register themselves.

        By default this scans the directory configured on the instance (``programs``
        unless overridden via ``Novax(programs=...)``). Every ``.py`` file under it is
        imported recursively; files whose name starts with ``_`` (e.g. ``__init__.py``)
        are skipped. A missing directory is ignored so the convention stays opt-in.
        Programs imported anywhere else are unaffected and still registered.

        Args:
            directory: Directory to scan recursively. Defaults to the instance's
                configured programs directory. When that is ``None`` (scanning
                disabled) this is a no-op.

        Returns:
            The list of all registered program IDs after the scan.
        """
        target = directory if directory is not None else self._programs
        if target is None:
            return []
        root = Path(target)
        if not root.is_dir():
            return []
        for py_file in sorted(root.rglob("*.py")):
            if py_file.name.startswith("_"):
                continue
            rel = py_file.relative_to(root).with_suffix("")
            module_name = "novax._scanned_programs." + ".".join(rel.parts)
            _import_module(str(py_file), name=module_name)
        return self.auto_register()
replacements.newString:
def scan_programs(self, directory: str | Path | None = None) -> list[str]:
        """Import every program module under a directory, then register all programs.

        By default this scans the directory configured on the instance (``programs``
        unless overridden via ``Novax(programs=...)``). Every ``.py`` file under it is
        imported recursively so its ``@nova.program`` functions self-register; files
        whose name starts with ``_`` (e.g. ``__init__.py``) are skipped. A missing or
        disabled (``None``) directory imports nothing, so the convention stays opt-in.

        Either way this finishes by calling :meth:`auto_register`, so programs imported
        anywhere else are picked up too and a single ``scan_programs()`` call is enough.

        Args:
            directory: Directory to scan recursively. Defaults to the instance's
                configured programs directory. When that is ``None`` nothing is imported.

        Returns:
            The list of all registered program IDs.
        """
        target = directory if directory is not None else self._programs
        if target is not None:
            root = Path(target)
            if root.is_dir():
                for py_file in sorted(root.rglob("*.py")):
                    if py_file.name.startswith("_"):
                        continue
                    rel = py_file.relative_to(root).with_suffix("")
                    module_name = "novax._scanned_programs." + ".".join(rel.parts)
                    _import_module(str(py_file), name=module_name)
        return self.auto_register()

diff --git a/tests/novax/test_auto_register.py b/tests/novax/test_auto_register.py
index 60921e0..34d0c3e 100644
--- a/tests/novax/test_auto_register.py
+++ b/tests/novax/test_auto_register.py
@@ -216,3 +216,107 @@ def test_import_module_file_path_reuses_cached_instance():
 def test_import_module_unknown_raises():
     with pytest.raises(ModuleNotFoundError):
         _import_module("tests.novax.does_not_exist_module")
+
+
+# --- Novax.scan_programs --------------------------------------------------
+
+
+def _write_program(directory: Path, filename: str, program_id: str) -> None:
+    """Create a ``.py`` file under ``directory`` defining a single @nova.program."""
+    directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
+    (directory / filename).write_text(
+        "import nova\n\n\n"
+        f"@nova.program(id={program_id!r})\n"
+        "async def prog(ctx: nova.ProgramContext):\n"
+        "    pass\n"
+    )
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_registers_files_in_directory(tmp_path):
+    programs_dir = tmp_path / "programs"
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "first.py", "scan_first")
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "second.py", "scan_second")
+
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=str(programs_dir))
+    registered = novax.scan_programs()
+
+    assert "scan_first" in registered
+    assert "scan_second" in registered
+    assert novax.program_manager.has_program("scan_first")
+    assert novax.program_manager.has_program("scan_second")
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_skips_underscore_files(tmp_path):
+    programs_dir = tmp_path / "programs"
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "visible.py", "scan_visible")
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "_hidden.py", "scan_hidden")
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "__init__.py", "scan_init")
+
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=str(programs_dir))
+    registered = novax.scan_programs()
+
+    assert "scan_visible" in registered
+    assert "scan_hidden" not in registered
+    assert "scan_init" not in registered
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_is_recursive(tmp_path):
+    programs_dir = tmp_path / "programs"
+    _write_program(programs_dir / "nested", "deep.py", "scan_deep")
+
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=str(programs_dir))
+    registered = novax.scan_programs()
+
+    assert "scan_deep" in registered
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_missing_directory_returns_empty(tmp_path):
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=str(tmp_path / "does_not_exist"))
+
+    assert novax.scan_programs() == []
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_disabled_when_programs_none(tmp_path):
+    programs_dir = tmp_path / "programs"
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "ignored.py", "scan_ignored")
+
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=None)
+
+    assert novax.scan_programs() == []
+    assert not novax.program_manager.has_program("scan_ignored")
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_directory_argument_overrides_default(tmp_path):
+    programs_dir = tmp_path / "custom"
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "custom.py", "scan_custom")
+
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=None)
+    registered = novax.scan_programs(programs_dir)
+
+    assert "scan_custom" in registered
+
+
+def test_scan_programs_keeps_imported_programs_registered(tmp_path):
+    # A program defined/imported outside the scanned directory.
+    @nova.program(id="scan_imported")
+    async def imported(ctx: nova.ProgramContext):
+        pass
+
+    programs_dir = tmp_path / "programs"
+    _write_program(programs_dir, "scanned.py", "scan_scanned")
+
+    novax = Novax(app_name="novax_scan_test", programs=str(programs_dir))
+    registered = novax.scan_programs()
+

@@ -197,6 +207,35 @@ For more information on using NOVAx see the [README](https://github.com/wandelbo
 
 > **Important:** When using NOVAx, you must import the actual program functions from their respective Python files. Only importing the program files won't suffice. This ensures proper function registration and execution within the NOVAx runtime environment.
 
+### Registering programs
+
+Programs register themselves with a global registry when decorated with `@nova.program`, so NOVAx just needs the modules that define them to be imported. There are three ways to get them registered, and they can be combined:
+
+- **Directory scanning (default):** NOVAx scans a `programs` directory and imports every `.py` file under it (recursively), so dropping a new file in `programs/` is enough — no manual import needed. Files whose name starts with `_` (e.g. `__init__.py`) are skipped, and a missing directory is ignored. Configure or disable it via the constructor:
+
+  ```python
+  from nova import Novax
+
+  Novax(app, programs="programs")  # default; scans ./programs
+  Novax(app, programs="my_pkg/robot_programs")  # custom directory
+  Novax(app, programs=None)  # disable scanning
+  ```
+
+- **Plain import:** any program imported elsewhere (inside or outside `programs/`) is still registered.
+
+  ```python
+  import my_pkg.special_program  # noqa: F401  (registers on import)
+  ```
+
+- **Explicit:** `novax.register_module("my_pkg.programs")` imports a module/file and registers its programs on demand.
+
+For local development you can serve everything without any FastAPI boilerplate:
+
+```bash
+novax run                       # scan ./programs and serve
+novax run my_pkg/one_program.py # also import a specific file, then scan ./programs
+```
+

=== serve.py ===
import asyncio

import nova
from examples.plan_and_execute import plan_and_execute


@nova.program(id="test1")
async def test(ctx: nova.ProgramContext):
    print("Hello, world!")


@nova.program(
    id="simple_program",
    name="Simple Program",
    description="Simple program that prints 'Hello World!' and then sleeps a bit.",
)
async def simple_program(ctx: nova.ProgramContext, number_of_steps: int = 30):
    """Simple program that prints "Hello World!" and then sleeps a bit."""
    print("Hello World!")

    for i in range(number_of_steps):
        print(f"Step: {i}")
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

    print("Finished Hello World!")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn

    from novax import Novax

    novax = Novax()
    app = novax.create_app()
    novax.include_programs_router(app)

    novax.register_program(test)
    novax.register_program(simple_program)
    novax.register_program(plan_and_execute)

    uvicorn.run(
        app,
        host="0.0.0.0",
        port=8000,
        reload=False,
        log_level="info",
        proxy_headers=True,
        forwarded_allow_ips="*",
    )

data.content:
All 29 tests pass. Here's the simplification I applied to [novax/novax.py](novax/novax.py):

**Before** — three call sites each did a scan followed by a separate `auto_register()`, and `scan_programs()` had two early-`return []` branches that skipped registration:

```python
# __init__
self.scan_programs()
self.include_programs_router(app)
self.auto_register()          # redundant second registry walk

# scan_programs
if target is None:
    return []                 # skips auto_register
root = Path(target)
if not root.is_dir():
    return []                 # skips auto_register
...
return self.auto_register()
```

**After** — `scan_programs()` always ends in `auto_register()` (it just imports nothing when scanning is disabled or the dir is missing), so callers need a single call:

```python
# __init__
self.include_programs_router(app)
self.scan_programs()          # one call does discovery + registration

# scan_programs
target = directory if directory is not None else self._programs
if target is not None:
    root = Path(target)
    if root.is_dir():
        for py_file in ...:
            _import_module(...)
return self.auto_register()    # always syncs the registry
```

Net effect:
- Removed the double registry walk in both `__init__` and `serve()`.
- One method (`scan_programs`) is now the single entry point; `auto_register` stays as the shared primitive (still used by `register_module` and public API).
- No behavior regression for `programs=None` + plain-import — that path still registers, because `auto_register()` always runs.

The mental model is now cleaner: **`scan_programs` = discover + register (one call)**, **`auto_register` = the register half, reused internally**.

toolRequests.arguments:
{"content": """"
This example shows how to use the Python SDK to control a virtual KUKA KR16 R2010 robot.

This demonstrates:
- Setting up a virtual robot controller
- Connecting to the robot
- Planning and executing basic movements
- Using joint and point-to-point motion types

Key robotics concepts:
- Motion groups: Controllable robot parts (usually the arm)
- TCP (Tool Center Point): The point you control on the robot
- Joint movement (jnt): Move by specifying joint angles in radians
- Point-to-point movement (ptp): Move to a specific position/orientation (x,y,z, rotation angle in radians)
- Pose: Position (x,y,z) and orientation (rx,ry,rz) in 3D space

Drop a file like this into the ``programs`` directory and NOVAx picks it up
automatically -- no manual import needed.
"""

import asyncio

import nova
from nova import api, run_program
from nova.actions import cartesian_ptp, circular, joint_ptp, linear
from nova.cell import virtual_controller
from nova.types import MotionSettings, Pose


@nova.program()
async def p2(ctx: nova.ProgramContext, count: int = 3):
    for i in range(count):
        print(f"Hello World {i + 1}/{count}")
        await asyncio.sleep(1)  # Simulate some work being done
    return "Hello World"


# Configure the robot program
@nova.program(
    id="start_here",  # Unique identifier of the program. If not provided, the function name will be used.
    name="Start Here",  # Readable name of the program
    # viewer=nova.viewers.Rerun(),  # add this line for a 3D visualization (headless in-cluster: leave disabled)
    preconditions=nova.ProgramPreconditions(
        controllers=[
            virtual_controller(
                name="kuka-kr16-r2010",
                manufacturer=api.models.Manufacturer.KUKA,
                type="kuka-kr16_r2010_2",
            )
        ],
        cleanup_controllers=False,
    ),
)
async def start(ctx: nova.ProgramContext):
    """Main robot control function."""
    cell = ctx.cell
    controller = await cell.controller("kuka-kr16-r2010")
    cycle = ctx.cycle(extra={"program": "start_here"})

    slow = MotionSettings(tcp_velocity_limit=50)

    motion_group = controller[0]
    home_joints = await motion_group.joints()
    tcp_names = await motion_group.tcp_names()
    tcp = tcp_names[0]

    # Get current TCP pose and create target poses
    current_pose = await motion_group.tcp_pose(tcp)
    # Define the target pose based on the current pose with an offset of 100 mm on the x-axis
    target_pose = current_pose @ Pose((100, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0))
    offset = 200

    # Actions define the sequence of movements and other actions to be executed by the robot
    actions = [
        joint_ptp(home_joints, settings=slow),  # Move to home position slowly
        cartesian_ptp(target_pose),  # Move to target pose
        joint_ptp(home_joints),  # Return to home
        cartesian_ptp(
            target_pose @ [offset, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
        ),  # Move 200mm in target pose's local x-axis
        joint_ptp(home_joints),
        linear(target_pose @ (offset, offset, 0, 0, 0, 0)),  # Move 200mm in local x and y axes
        joint_ptp(home_joints, settings=slow),
        cartesian_ptp(target_pose @ Pose((0, offset, 0, 0, 0, 0))),
        joint_ptp(home_joints),
        circular(
            target_pose @ Pose((0, 200, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
            intermediate=target_pose @ Pose((200, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
        ),
        joint_ptp(home_joints),
    ]

    # Start the cycle
    await cycle.start()

    # Plan the movements (shows in 3D viewer or creates an rrd file)
    joint_trajectory = await motion_group.plan(actions, tcp)

    # OPTIONAL: Execute the planned movements
    # You can comment out the lines below to only see the plan in Rerun
    print("Executing planned movements...")
    await motion_group.execute(joint_trajectory, tcp, actions=actions)

    # Finish the cycle
    await cycle.finish()
    print("Movement execution completed!")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_program(start)
", "filePath": "/Users/cbiering/home/dev/wandelbots/wandelbots-nova/examples/your-nova-app/programs/start_here.py"}

    offset = 200

    # Actions define the sequence of movements and other actions to be executed by the robot
    actions = [
        joint_ptp(home_joints, settings=slow),  # Move to home position slowly
        cartesian_ptp(target_pose),  # Move to target pose
        joint_ptp(home_joints),  # Return to home
        cartesian_ptp(
            target_pose @ [offset, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
        ),  # Move 200mm in target pose's local x-axis
        joint_ptp(home_joints),
        linear(target_pose @ (offset, offset, 0, 0, 0, 0)),  # Move 200mm in local x and y axes
        joint_ptp(home_joints, settings=slow),
        cartesian_ptp(target_pose @ Pose((0, offset, 0, 0, 0, 0))),
        joint_ptp(home_joints),
        circular(
            target_pose @ Pose((0, 200, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
            intermediate=target_pose @ Pose((200, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
        ),
        joint_ptp(home_joints),
    ]

    # Start the cycle
    await cycle.start()

    # Plan the movements (shows in 3D viewer or creates an rrd file)
    joint_trajectory = await motion_group.plan(actions, tcp)

    # OPTIONAL: Execute the planned movements
    # You can comment out the lines below to only see the plan in Rerun
    print("Executing planned movements...")
    await motion_group.execute(joint_trajectory, tcp, actions=actions)

    # Finish the cycle
    await cycle.finish()
    print("Movement execution completed!")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_program(start)

data.content:
Fixed. The Dependency review job failed because the new [examples/your-nova-app/uv.lock](examples/your-nova-app/uv.lock) locked the **critical**-severity `python-statemachine@3.0.0` (GHSA-v4jc-pm6r-3vj8 / CVE-2026-47103 — SCXML `eval()` injection).

Root cause: the app was pinned to SDK `5.7.4`, which hard-pins `python-statemachine==3.0.0`. The patched version is `3.2.0` (which the main repo already uses). I re-locked the example, bumping `wandelbots-nova 5.7.4 → 5.7.6`, which pulls the patched `python-statemachine 3.0.0 → 3.2.0`.

- `uv lock --check` passes (lock is consistent).
- No `3.0.0` statemachine entries remain; it's now `3.2.0`.
- That was the only package the action flagged, so the check should now go green.

Two notes:
- The **Node 20 deprecation** line is just a non-blocking warning emitted by `actions/dependency-review-action@v4`'s runtime — not the failure cause, and nothing in this repo controls it.
- Reminder of the earlier coordination point: the app now both uses `programs/` scanning **and** locks SDK `5.7.6` — but scanning ships only in *this* PR, so `Novax(app)` on the published `5.7.6` won't register the moved program. The template becomes fully functional once this PR is released; at that point bump the floor in [pyproject.toml](examples/your-nova-app/pyproject.toml) and re-lock. Want me to add a `TODO` there now so it isn't forgotten?

replacements.newString:
This template contains a simple python app served by [fastapi](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi) provided via NOVAx.
It shows you how to use the [NOVA Python SDK](https://github.com/wandelbotsgmbh/wandelbots-nova) and build a basic app with it.

Robot programs live in the `programs/` directory — every `@nova.program` there is
discovered and registered automatically, so adding a new program is just dropping a
`.py` file in `programs/` (no manual import needed).

Use the following steps for development:

* make sure you have `uv` installed
    * you can follow these steps https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/
* ensure proper environment variables are set in `.env`
    * note: you might need to set/update `NOVA_ACCESS_TOKEN` and `NOVA_API`
* use `uv run app` to run the server
    * access the docs on `http://localhost:3000/docs`
* build, push and install the app with `nova app install`
replacements.oldString:
This template contains a simple python app served by [fastapi](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi) provided via NOVAx.
It shows you how to use the [NOVA Python SDK](https://github.com/wandelbotsgmbh/wandelbots-nova) and build a basic app with it.

Use the following steps for development:

* make sure you have `uv` installed
    * you can follow these steps https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/
* ensure proper environment variables are set in `.env`
    * note: you might need to set/update `NOVA_ACCESS_TOKEN` and `NOVA_API`
* use `uv run app` to run the server
    * access the docs on `http://localhost:8000/docs`
* build, push and install the app with `nova app install`

replacements.newString:
## quick run (no app needed)

To iterate on a program from dev without scaffolding an app, use the `novax` CLI. With
no arguments it scans the `programs/` directory; you can also point it at a specific
file — either way the `@nova.program` functions auto-register:

```bash
uv run novax run --cell cell                          # scan ./programs and serve
uv run novax run programs/start_here.py --cell cell   # or serve a specific file
```
replacements.oldString:
## quick run (no app needed)

To iterate on a program from dev without scaffolding an app, just point the
`novax` CLI at any file with `@nova.program` functions — they auto-register:

```bash
uv run novax run your_nova_app/start_here.py --cell cell
```

questions.question: The app can't use the unreleased programs_dir API against the published SDK. How should I resolve it?
questions.message:
**Option A — Build the example against the local branch SDK (proper dev loop).**
Expand the Skaffold build context to the repo root and add a `[tool.uv.sources]` path pointing at the in-repo SDK, so the image is built with *your working-tree* SDK (scanning included). Reflects local edits immediately. Downside: the example's Dockerfile/skaffold become monorepo-aware (less "standalone template"), and would be reverted/adjusted at release.

**Option B — Keep the template on the released SDK for now (works immediately).**
Revert the entrypoint to the explicit-import registration (`import ...start_here`) that works on 5.7.6, keep the SDK scanning feature + tests in the PR, and switch the template to `programs_dir` scanning once the SDK is released (bump the version floor then). Programs can still live in `programs/`, just imported explicitly for now.

**Option C — Pin the SDK to this branch via a uv git source.** Only works if every SDK change is committed+pushed (uncommitted edits like the `programs_dir` rename wouldn't be picked up), so the dev loop is awkward.

options.label: Option A — build against local branch SDK
options.description: Real dev loop; restructures the example build to be monorepo-aware.

options.label: Option B — revert template to released-SDK explicit import
options.description: Works now; re-adopt scanning after SDK release.

options.label: Option C — uv git source to the branch

## quick run (no app needed)

To iterate on a program from dev without scaffolding an app, use the `novax` CLI. With
no arguments it scans the `programs/` directory; you can also point it at a specific
file — either way the `@nova.program` functions auto-register:

```bash
uv run novax run --cell cell                          # scan ./programs and serve
uv run novax run programs/start_here.py --cell cell   # or serve a specific file
```

replacements.oldString:
from pathlib import Path

import uvicorn
from decouple import config
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse, HTMLResponse

from nova import Novax

CELL_ID = config("CELL_ID", default="cell", cast=str)
BASE_PATH = config("BASE_PATH", default="", cast=str)
PROGRAMS_DIR = "programs"

replacements.newString:
# Include the programs router and register every @nova.program. NOVAx scans the
# ``programs`` directory and imports each file, so dropping a new program into
# ``programs/`` is enough -- no manual import required.
# See https://github.com/wandelbotsgmbh/wandelbots-nova/blob/main/README.md#novax for more information
novax = Novax(app, programs_dir="programs")
replacements.oldString:
# Register every @nova.program under ./programs. Dropping a new .py file into
# programs/ is enough -- no manual import required. Files starting with "_" are
# skipped. (Once the SDK release ships directory scanning, this whole block
# becomes ``Novax(app, programs_dir=PROGRAMS_DIR)``.)
# See https://github.com/wandelbotsgmbh/wandelbots-nova/blob/main/README.md#novax for more information
novax = Novax(app)
for program_file in sorted(Path(PROGRAMS_DIR).glob("*.py")):
    if not program_file.name.startswith("_"):
        novax.register_module(str(program_file))

arguments.explanation: Update README to reflect programs living in the package with explicit import, noting scanning will follow.

replacements.newString:
This template contains a simple python app served by [fastapi](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi) provided via NOVAx.
It shows you how to use the [NOVA Python SDK](https://github.com/wandelbotsgmbh/wandelbots-nova) and build a basic app with it.

Robot programs are defined with `@nova.program` (see `your_nova_app/start_here.py`) and
registered by importing their module in `your_nova_app/register_programs.py`. Add a new
program by creating its module and importing it there. (Automatic directory scanning
will follow once that SDK feature is released.)

Use the following steps for development:
replacements.oldString:
This template contains a simple python app served by [fastapi](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi) provided via NOVAx.
It shows you how to use the [NOVA Python SDK](https://github.com/wandelbotsgmbh/wandelbots-nova) and build a basic app with it.

Robot programs live in the `programs/` directory — every `@nova.program` there is
discovered and registered automatically, so adding a new program is just dropping a
`.py` file in `programs/` (no manual import needed).

Use the following steps for development:

replacements.newString:
```bash
uv run novax run your_nova_app/start_here.py --cell cell
```
replacements.oldString:
```bash
uv run novax run programs/start_here.py --cell cell
```

data.content:
Works on the released SDK — both programs register, lint clean. `skaffold dev` will now boot without the `programs_dir` crash.

Reverted to the original explicit-import route:
- Moved `start_here.py` back into the package → [your_nova_app/start_here.py](examples/your-nova-app/your_nova_app/start_here.py); removed the `programs/` directory.
- [register_programs.py](examples/your-nova-app/your_nova_app/register_programs.py): `import your_nova_app.start_here  # noqa` + `Novax(app)`, with a `TODO` comment that directory scanning (`Novax(app, programs_dir="programs")`) will follow once released. Reload dir back to `["/app/your_nova_app"]`.
- [Dockerfile](examples/your-nova-app/Dockerfile): dropped `COPY programs programs`.
- [skaffold.yaml](examples/your-nova-app/skaffold.yaml): sync back to `your_nova_app/**/*.py` only.
- [README.md](examples/your-nova-app/README.md): describes the import-based registration and notes scanning will follow; quick-run points at `your_nova_app/start_here.py`.

The SDK-side scanning feature (`programs_dir`, `scan_programs`, CLI, tests) stays in the PR — the template will adopt it after release.
